speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This conversation with Marie Leitch Fisher took place at her home in Lewiston on October 29, 1975.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How do you wind up there in the mid -20s when you came?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: Well, I had a cousin who lived there and I guess I just applied for the job and got it and went up. Those days you'd write applications telling what your qualifications were, what you could teach, and that's all I did and I got the job.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where were you living at the time?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: Well, my folks were living at Neskhurst, but I was only there during the summers, so I didn't really live much of anywhere. Wherever my trunk was, that's where I lived.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you talk before you came to Lewiston?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: Oh yes, I talked. Oh, I must have talked 10 or 15 years, I guess, before I went to Lewiston. No, I hadn't talked that long. I taught about eight years before I went there, so I had experience and pretty good recommendations, I guess, because I got the job without any trouble. I taught primary for four years, and then they transferred me to the fifth and sixth, and then I taught the seventh and eighth, so I taught all of them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is this all in Bovill  or before you came?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: No, in Bovill, but I taught rural schools and two -room schools before I went to Bovill.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh yes, I was going to ask you too, now you got your teacher training in Lewiston?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: In the normal over here, yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How long did that take in those days?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: Well, when I started out, all you had to do was go to summer school, which lasted, I think, nine weeks, something like that, and you got what was called a third grade certificate. Then you'd teach that on that third grade certificate that year, and then you'd go back to summer school, and then you got a second grade, and then you'd go back to summer school next year, and you'd get a first grade, and then you had your certificate.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But the people that wanted to go to school, if they'd take a whole year at once, why they didn't have to go through that?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: I couldn't be bothered going a whole year, and I could go nine weeks and get a job.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, what was the training itself like? Did they prepare you? Was it, do you think, very different than the way they do the teaching now?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: I don't know how they teach now. From the papers I pick up that the kids drop on the way home, they don't teach like they used to. From these papers I pick up that the kids go by and they've got an A on them, and I look through them, and there's a whole lot of mistakes on them. They can't spell, and they can't write, they've got big printing. And when I taught school, the kids went to the board and learned to write. They never did print, but of course, before I quit teaching, they had started printing. But I never bothered with that, because I couldn't see the value of printing first, then writing. And we had to teach Palmer Method of writing those days.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you were trained at Lewiston for teaching at the Normal, was the training very specific? Do they teach you just how to teach in a classroom, or what?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: Yes, but they didn't.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: They taught the way it should be in a classroom, but after you got in a classroom, you had to remember what they told you, and then twist it to fit the, because these people that were training us were dedicated. I don't think that many of them had ever done very much rural school teaching, where you had eight grades in one room. And we had special courses in teaching English, and special courses in arithmetic, and special teachers to teach us how to teach spelling and everything else. But after you get out and start doing it while you just forget all that and do it your own way, that is, if you've got anything up here. What you're saying is it was more, it was theory and not practice. That's right. But I wasn't a bad primary teacher. By the time Christmas came, the kids could pick out different words. The smart ones, of course, the idiots, the morons, and the little people on the curve, you know, they couldn't. But the average kid knew a few words that they could pick out of the paper, and I'd never let them learn by the picture on the paper. They had to know the words because I taught phonics, and they could read pretty good. If you don't believe it, I asked Joe Holland. I had his kids in first grade. And Junior, his oldest boy, the one that got killed in the Army, he could almost read by Christmas. And by the time school was out, he was a pretty good reader. But he was a smart kid. But there were a lot of them because if a kid has phonics, he can set his own pace. He'd have to go around and wait for this sight reading. I don't know how I ever got kids to learn to read when I didn't teach phonics, but I must have. But now they're going back to phonics, somebody told me.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: I've heard that too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I'm just wondering, did you have any special methods that you found that worked to get kids interested in reading? Or was it strict drills and that sort of thing?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: No.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you get, are there any games in that type of story?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: Oh, sure. You know, that story of the three Billy Goats, the kids would read the story and then they'd act out. They'd be the three Billy Goats trying to get across the bridge that the troll was on. And then when you were teaching him how to, manners, why you'd have a kid knock at the door and the other kid would open the door and he'd come in blah blah blah. And I didn't do that very often, but months and a while we'd have things like that. And of course on Friday, afternoons, why they got a lot of artwork and spelling matches. Send them up the board and see what kid, give them a bunch of figures and see who could get through it quick. But those days we used to have contests between our school and dairy. Not only in athletics, but in spelling and arithmetic and stuff. Spelling and arithmetic and context were the only two contests we had. And they were fun because they just were.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were these frequent get togethers for contests with theory? I mean, it was a common thing?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: Well, it was for a year or two. It all depended greatly on who the teachers were. We found a got the point where we didn't go down, except they would have their track needs. But we had a superintendent that believed in developing their mental ability as well as their muscles. So we had them then. Of course, the dairy superintendent, I don't even remember who he was, cooperated. Of course, it only happened probably once a year, but then the kids got a lot of fun preparing for it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it the same kind of thing in those days that nowadays you really do get the feeling that athletics is more important than schooling a lot of the time? Was it that way then too? Was athletics stressed so much?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: Not in our schools.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: The idea was to get through all those books and pass into the next grade, and you didn't do that out playing basketball. Of course, I don't know about high school, but they were very basketball minded. And of course, everybody liked athletics. They never had enough kids to have a football team, but they always had good basketball teams, girl teams, some boy teams. And Boho had the prettiest girls in the whole Laytop County. I've heard that before.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That's really true, huh?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: Yes, it really is. They were just awfully...well, they were above average for good looks. We only had one or two fat ones, and even the fat ones were nice looking in the face. But the Lancaster, the Phillips, I don't have any pictures around with those girls, but they...oh, I've got them somewhere. They'd take a month to dig them out of this trash. But they were good looking, yeah. Beautiful girls. And the boys were nice looking, but the girls were better looking.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: Well, that's good. That's the way it should be. Oh, sure.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you first came to Boville, were all the primary grades together as one, or were there different classes?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: Oh, they had the first and second grade together, in third and fourth grade, fifth and sixth, seventh and eighth. There were four grade teachers, and there were, I guess, three or four high school teachers.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was this an improvement for you as a teacher to be able to just teach two grades instead of teaching a whole eight in one grade?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: Well, it was a lot easier. I'll tell you that.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: You didn't have half the work to do, and it was more fun in that you could... You didn't have to have one kid teaching another. You could do it all yourself.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: I don't know. I enjoyed some of those one -room schools a lot, too, though. Especially one out here out in Lewiston somewhere. Someday I'm going to take the car and go out and see if I can find that school. You went out to Web Bridge, or you went out to that web post office, and you went up in those hills somewhere, and there's two roads after you leave Web Store, and I don't know which one I went on.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: I'm surprised that you enjoyed the one -room school. I would think that for a teacher to deal with eight grades at once must just about be the limit. But listen, I didn't find it in Volvo the first eight years I taught there.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: But if you're the teacher in a one -room school, boy, you are somebody the kids respect you. They never tried the smart, aliki things these kids in town tried. Why, if I'd been teaching anywhere and the kids had talked back to me like they do to these people now, I'd have broken their necks. Because those days you could spank kids. Of course, sometimes some of the people didn't like it very well because their little darlings got spanked, but they couldn't do anything about it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But why?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: I wouldn't teach school now for all the tea in China.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did each teacher have to develop her own way of dealing with the problems of kids rebelling or not paying attention to you?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: Yes.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: There was never any rebellion in those one -room schools because school was fun for most of those country kids. They got away from home and they got in with other kids, and they enjoyed school.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you feel that you had to be very strict with them?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: No. Not the way you do in a town school. I know when I'd had seven or eight years experience before I went to Volvo, so I said, I usually tell them the first morning how this was school, and that I knew all their tricks. If they had been good, I'd be good. If they were going to be mean, I knew lots more mean ways than they did. I got it across that what I said was going to be, that was it. So I had very little trouble with the primary grades or the other ones either. I know I got awful mad at the superintendent one time because he spanked one of my kids. I felt if the kid needed spanking, I was the one to spank him, but the superintendent did it. He didn't make any friends influence any people either. He only stated that in all that year he left because the kid didn't need a spanking in the first place. And if he needed one, I was the one to give it to him, but I didn't get the chance prof did it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I imagine that he must have, that being a teacher in a school, well you will first, you were saying before that the first seven or eight years in Boville, you say there was no discipline problem?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: No, the first seven or eight years when I was teaching in the country schools, there was never any discipline problem.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: Did it take you a matter of time to establish yourself in Boville as a...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Disponarian?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: No, as a person that was going to be there, someone that was, you say the teacher didn't have the same kind of status in a town as she did in the country.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: Oh, well the little tiny kids in school in Boville, they hadn't got to the point yet, and I don't know whether the upper grades had, that they could do as they pleased. They came to school because school was where they were supposed to be, and I told them it was just like their daddy went to work every morning and they went to school, the same thing.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: And, but there wasn't any discipline problem in the primary grades in Boville. And I was, I was big, and it makes a difference, you know. We had one little gal come out from New England to teach in Boville in high school. And she was just a little shrimp. I bet she didn't weigh more than 90 pounds soaking wet. And those kids, I went down the basement one day, and she was down in the basement crying. And I asked her what was the matter, and she said the kids were giving her a bad time. I don't know what they'd done. And I stormed up into that high school room, and I gave those kids the hardest bawling out you ever saw. And here I was teaching primary downstairs, but I'd had these kids, and here they were giving her a bad time. And I told, I don't know if it helped any, but anybody could clear out from Maine to teach a bunch of hoodlums. And they weren't hoodlums, but they were just acting like it. And one teacher we had was a little shorty, and she's teaching in California now about ready to retire. They picked her up one day and set her in the wastebasket just because she was small. Well, they wouldn't have tried that with me. They wouldn't have dared in the first place because I just looked like they'd better not try it, you know. And I think it makes a lot of difference. You know, some people have a Mickey Mouse, and I'm afraid of you, and I never looked like I was afraid of them. I may have been scared to death inside, but they didn't know it. And I guess...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What made you decide to become a teacher in the first place?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: Oh, I don't think I decided. My mother was a teacher, and one of... I guess that made...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did that made me teach?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: I don't remember. Well, anyway, when my mother was raising me, the only thing a woman could do was to teach school or be a nurse. And of course, they didn't think nursing was a... My mother was a very puritanical gal, so she just convinced me. Well, I didn't think much about it, so I became a teacher, kind of shoved into it without any thought. But my cousin, her folks let her be a... take a commercial course. And she had... I don't know, I'm better off than she is at that, even if I didn't teach. Teaching wasn't bad those days, but I wouldn't do it again. It seems not the kind of kids these people are raising.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: Seems like...

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: It seems like most women, of course, didn't decide to become teachers or nurses. I mean, most women grew up, got married, and then that was it, and they were homemakers. So in that sense, it always seemed to me that the women who were teachers were different than most women.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: Well, maybe I was. And the first school I taught, I was in the country out of Kami 'i. And the man was such a tyrant and so mean that I thought, oh, I'm never going to get married if that's what it's like. Of course, our home life, my mother had... it was perfect. And I'd never seen any woman treated like she was, and I thought, oh, nobody's ever going to get a brand new. So I taught for quite a while, and finally I got married.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This man was the one that ran the school?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: No. He was just somebody I had... I had to board somewhere. That was the meanest man I ever saw.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: Were women able to... I know that the men, it seems like the men were running things even more than they do now in those days.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And were teachers supposed to keep their place and not speak their mind too much when it came to the running of things? Or were they able to speak out?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: Well, I think they could speak out if they wanted to.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: I never... but I wasn't very forward or brash. I didn't bother to go to the council meetings. And they didn't have a PTA in Bovil, so only about one year, about the time I quit teaching. So I didn't... In Bovil, all the teachers did was teach school, and then go to parties they were asked to.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: That was... Bovil was quite a partying town, like card parties, I mean.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: But I think the women could... teachers could say what they wanted to. I don't know.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: When you say card...

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: the card parties were frequent, were there a pattern to...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: were there certain groups that gave them? Or people's homes?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: Or what was the... Well, there were card clubs. There were... See, there was an afternoon bridge club and a night bridge club. And there were about three Pino club clubs. And then every once in a while, different lodges in town would have a party and get the hall and have the whole town in. Twenty tables, maybe something like that. Maybe not twenty tables. Ten or twelve tables. I forgot. And then once in a while, the different lodges would decide they'd have a dance. And they had a dance hall there, and there were dances every week. The teachers were allowed to go to all that. They were not... told they couldn't. Although I stayed with one woman and said I shouldn't go to dances, but I went anyway.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were there dances for townspeople, Muslim?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: There were for everybody in town, neighboring towns too. People had come from Derry and Elk River.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would Lumberjacks put those too?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: Oh, yeah.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: The... the young ones would. And because everybody had lived in Louisville, worked in the woods, except a few of the... ...own people that had stores. But the... all the young fellows worked in the woods, and they'd come to the dances.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: I was thinking more of the... of the Lumberjacks that weren't local, but lived in the camps. Oh, the ones that had...

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: that liked to dance and could get into town, they came.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So you say there was a dance just about every week in the year of the day?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: Well, I can't remember whether they were that often or not, but they'd be at least... ...they'd probably have... had them twice a month. And during the Depression, we had... we had dances, I think, about every week down in an old building. But... had been a church and then a store. And the locals, one of them would play a Jews' harp... or a mouth harp. One would play the piano and one would play the drums. I don't think we had a violin, but we'd have a lot of fun. Pass the hat for... to pay the musicians, and they'd probably get maybe a dollar apiece. Quarters and nickels would be all they'd ever get. And we'd wind up at somebody's house for... sandwiches and coffee afterward.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And these go away into the night?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: Oh, oh, they'd... they'd like... You know, those days you had to... there was a law that you had to stop, I think, at twelve. And I guess you could... Oh, you either had to stop at twelve or one.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was it, Dwight, that the dancers used to have to stop at Gery?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: No, I used to go to one sometimes, mostly at twelve. But we... anyway, it was a law you had to stop. And then we'd go somewhere and eat, and maybe we'd get home at two or three in the morning, it all depended.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Somebody would put on a feed at their home first?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: Yes, just with the dance. Sandwiches and stuff.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And this was pretty much open to everybody who was at the dance?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: Well, no, it'd just be the group that... Oh, six or eight of us couples would go. Of course, there'd be probably three or four different people having their refreshments afterward. Different homes, but... About... Six or eight couples of us always wound up afterward. And I suppose there were six or eight, ten other couples wound up somewhere else. Of course, those people we didn't know much about. I mean, we didn't... Oh, you know how it is, you have your own group and stay in it.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: Was your group mostly people that...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: was this after you were married?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: Yeah, before and after.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were they people that your husband knew or that you had known for...?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: Well, he knew them, of course, before I did, but then I knew them after. I said...

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: This card... the card playing now, Bovil seems a little different than other towns. I haven't heard particularly that playing cards was a real widespread in a lot of these towns. It was in Bovil.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: That was... I don't know, some of those... I guess there's one card club still running. I think it's three table club. And we had... Our bridge clubs, I think, were just two tables because people, some of them, never learned to play very good bridge. But we played... The three of us went over to Moscow to hear that fellow from Oregon come up and teach us how to play.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Play bridge?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: Mm -hmm.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: He's dead now, I think, and his system's gone up, but it was called... his name was... He had a system. He called Horse Saints Bridge. And he cut it down to just... Just the bare bones of bridge and it was... Well, and this man, Charles Goran, he's very much like it only Goran goes into a lot of Goran's books about that thick. And his was just about that, just a little paperback book. And he said everything Goran said. But Goran puts in a lot more fat. A lot more... Bids like Blackwood and... Oh, you know, if I say... If I'm an old trump, you say two clubs. That stuff, you know. He had a lot more conventions. Well, was that the main game that people played, or were there other games that were more popular in the... An old P -Knuckle was more widespread than Bridge, but... Our Bridge clubs lasted forever. As long as I was there, they were still playing. And then after four or five of us left town, there weren't enough people left to have a Bridge club, so it died out. One P -Knuckle club's still going. I guess, towards the last I heard. But there's a different group in Bobo now, there. I am up there, I only see three or four people that were there when I was... Lloyd Hall still there, and Mrs. McDonald, and Earl Crane. Sophie Crane, or Earl and Julius Crane.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: And... Candlers and Woods. And Yangles, that's about all that's left from what used to be there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there a lot of church activity too, when you first came?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: Oh yes, they had... When I first went there, they had a minister, but he... And for two or three years after I got there, they did, but the... They didn't have the church... Finally, it was without a minister for quite a while. But the Sunday School always dragged along. I was superintendent of the Sunday School Secretary, and name it, I was it. And the Sunday School. My husband used to say, you don't want to go fishing early Sunday night. I'd say, well, I'm not here. See, there are your kids waiting for you to go home for. I wasn't really, yes, but somebody had to do it. When I came down here, I decided I'd fund my... But the other guy did it now.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was this Sunday School for one of the town churches, or for...?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: It was Presbyterian. You see, there were two churches, the Catholic and the Presbyterian. And later on, the Nazarenes came in. But they're gone now, and I noticed they've got a little Baptist deal started.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: Nell told me that originally it was a community church, and everybody used it.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: And then there was a tiff about Catholics using it, and they wound up building their own. I never heard of that tiff between the Catholics. I thought the trouble was when some woman came in with a tent and held a revival. I think her name was Lake, or his name was Lake. But that just about died down by the time I'd gotten there, but the damage had been done. But I don't think they ever made any trouble about the Catholics.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why was there a problem about the revival?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: I don't know. I don't know what happened.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: That was way before my time. It was just about forgotten when I got there. But it happened early on.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: There were a lot of revivals in the country during those early years, I know.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: The church did a free wedding once for someone that could afford to...

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: Well, they always... I don't think they ever charge for weddings.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: Well, I think there was a whole wedding was put on for a bride.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the story about that?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: Well, all the years I was in Louisville, there had only been one wedding in that church, and that was one of the hall girls married somebody. And then this young girl that was an orphan normal, she has a father, but he... She was sort of abandoned. I was coming down the street one day, and I was active in the church, of course. They asked me if they could get married in the church. I said, well, I'll have to ask the elders if you can. So I did, and they said, yes. So we just... The fudge -fuss women and the ladies, they decided we just handled a throw -a -wedding form.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: But then, as far as that goes, it didn't cost us anything. We got the church for free, and... I made the cake, and another gal frosted it. That was all that was done. But it was a nice wedding. The girl had a wedding dress and everything. I don't know why we did it. Public relations, maybe. I don't know. And since then, there have been two or three weddings in the church, but I thought it was funny. I'd been there to a couple of funerals, and one wedding, and the church sat there. We had cleaned the church up pretty good, and had new windows in it. The windows aren't pretty like they were. We did it, though. They didn't get the pretty paper, and they didn't take care of it. Care of it. After they were on. Well, what I was thinking about the wedding was that it sounded nice, almost as if women decided to be a family for this girl, and didn't really have a wife. And I don't remember who gave her away. I can't remember much about it, because maybe nobody did. And one of her friends had gotten married and loaned her the wedding dress. And I don't remember. She had dried things. Best man and all that. Oh, that was quite an affair. And that started the ball rolling. And a year or two later, somebody else got married in the church.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were people getting married at home then?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: No. They'd just go over to Jesus the Peace or somebody and get married. There weren't any church weddings. Well, once in a while, there'd be a wedding in the Catholic church. But not many. I don't even remember two Catholic weddings.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: But then there may have been more.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: Well, when you first came to Louisville, you were single and going to teach. And I know that you had to find a place to live. And I've had the feeling, when you talked to me before, that that was really something of a problem. It was where a person could live, being single in a town. I had a cousin there, so I started staying with her.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: So I stayed with her all until in February. I went to a desk one night. And when I, a fellow that brought me home, she said the next day, I couldn't allow him in my house. And he was a very nice fellow. There wasn't anything wrong with him. And I don't know. And then we, she had her brother boarding there, too. And I was boarding there. And something happened. So I decided to move. So I moved up on the other end of town with a young couple. And started to have a real good time. Because they were young and we had card games and we had parties. And I did tell her, though, that I couldn't stay where they made home brew because I was a teacher. But I guess they made it and I didn't know it. It was just the same. But they didn't. They didn't inform you of it, so you didn't have to go on. So I didn't know it. But I kind of think that they did. I know they'd made it before I got there. But before I moved in, I'm not sure they made it then. But I wouldn't be surprised. They're both dead now, so I'll never find out. And I stayed there that year. And I think I stayed there the next year. And then I stayed one year with the pierces. And the next year I moved in the hotel and had a room and they'd been with meals at the restaurant. And around, there was one place that went every Monday night because they had chili. And another place they invited me every Friday night because they had some...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Homes?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: Homes, yeah. And then I, but Nell Smith had me down to eat several times. She had me so slow that I was late getting back to school.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: You just ate around, heard the kids ask you to, but I ate at the hotel. I went to the restaurant. The hotel had a dining room when I first stayed there. And then the dining room closed and I ate down at Guo Tzu's restaurant. It's not a lot snow.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: I don't know who it was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that the one next to the firehouse?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: Oh, yes. I'd go in there and eat my lunch when I'm in Bovill.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: Well, that's where I went for my breakfast and lunch and dinner. And if I... I used to have a soda fountain that you could get. Good milkshakes. I used to sometimes... For my lunch, I'd have a milkshake instead of a whole dinner. But usually I made it down. I'd talk to the restaurant every day.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You said there was one place that the lady would let you stay?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: Oh, they had...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know where the old opera house is in Bovill?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: That big house that's there, that two -story and a half house.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that right by Main Street there?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: Well, after you make the curve.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: She had a burning house and rooms. And I went... Of course, I hadn't been in town very long, but somebody said it was a burning house. So I stopped there one day and asked if I could get my meals there. Because I was staying at the hotel. She said, no, this is just for men. Strictly men. She wouldn't have any women from the place. That was old Lady David. She was an old French lady in her hair. It was just as black as gold. But she wouldn't let me eat there. I didn't ask to stay to room there. I just wanted to board there. No, it was just a strictly man. So she wouldn't let anybody eat there but men. That's funny. Well, perhaps she had all... Perhaps her table was full because those days... Then I think she just served the stuff family -style. She only had chairs enough for that many. I thought, well, the French are supposed to be good cooks. That'd be a good place to eat. But it didn't work out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you were living at the hotel, did you get to see Pat Malone very much?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: Oh, yes. He lived on the long hall and I lived on the front hall. I'd see him every day.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: He'd be sitting around the lobby and maybe I'd go down and sit by him, wait till the stage came in with evening papers to see who got off the stage. Or the bus, whatever it was. I think we called it stage in the early days. Yeah, I knew Pat very well.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he spend a lot of his time in the lobby there?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: Oh, yes.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: Because he roomed upstairs and there wasn't any place else to sit. Then he stayed in the lobby and smoked his pipe.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: There wasn't any other place to stay except in the pool halls.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: If you're... So the lobby was... And then the next people at both hotels made the lobby into a... I watched a part of it made a barbershop out of it, so it was a place where people sat around a lot. But after the barbershop went in there, I didn't go down much.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Which hotel was this?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: The one that they tore down, the Spokane. Nothing left there but a big gaping hole.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did Pat used to sit in it? Did he have a chair of his own that he sit in pretty much?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: Well, there were about four big heavy leather chairs. You know, all these old -fashioned kind. Brown, kind of like... not a light brown, but a pretty brown. And there were four of them along there. And they had... Some of them had wooden arms. And the men had sat there and scratched their matches on the arms. And there were holes about that deep where the matches had scratched like the pipes. And I don't think... And then in front of the windows were two overstuffed chairs. Pat had sat in one of those and looked up and down the street. Well, he wasn't sitting in the other. But there were two chairs in front of the window. And then there were... three or four went this way in the lobby. And there was a big sofa. There wasn't a sofa. I don't know what you'd call it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Santee maybe?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: It had these kind of cushions, only they were leather too. So there was room for all 10 or 15, 20 to sit around in there if they wanted to. That was a good hotel at one time. Big money.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: There were often little confabs in that lobby of people.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I mean, was it used as a gathering place?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: No, just people that were nothing around. It was just a loafers place.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So men mostly, huh?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: Mostly men. Because there weren't very many women living in the hotel. For years there was just the proprietor and me and one of the gals that worked at the restaurant. And then they had a couple of apartments on the third floor. And occasionally, so we'd go down and sit in the lobby. But not very often. There were interesting things to do.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: Well, did you talk to Pat enough to...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Can you tell me a little of what you remember him being like?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: I've heard a lot about him from different people. He's such an interesting old timer to me. I've seen pictures of him where he really is such a distinguished looking fellow, you know, big.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: Well, I don't know. He was a jolly old fellow and nothing ever seemed to worry him. And he... I can't remember being in any deep conversations with him, just to chat a little. Because there usually was... Well, I never did get him off by himself to talk to because I just see him in the lobby or meet him in the hall. But he got up earlier and I did and he was downstairs earlier than I. But he was... Everybody liked him and they sort of laughed at him too because he was supposed to have been quite a law man back in Wisconsin.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Duluth?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: Yeah, Duluth, Minnesota, someplace or other. And I never did find out how he happened to wind up in Bowmill of all places.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But what made him come out there?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: Well, you know that some of the lumber towns were... had a reputation for being pretty rough. And from what I've heard about Bowmill, its rough days were rather early and was quite a settled town by the twenties. Yet that was the guy who was in charge of keeping law and order and I heard he really didn't do much in that line. Well, there wasn't much to do because they were pretty law abiding citizens.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: All the bootleggers had come in but then they were pretty slick about their... peddling their wares and there wasn't much law to enforce.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: Kids, you know, a lot of towns, you have trouble with kids playing hooky and running away and all that. No Bowmill kids ever ran away from home so there's no need to hunt them.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: And there wasn't much to do.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: But the Hubert Hall was the town cop after Pat died. He didn't have much trouble either. Because there wasn't...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: what did I say?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: There wasn't much crying around Bowmill.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you used to hear stories that they would tell on Pat?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: It seems like nowadays there's lots of stories and jokes about him. Well...

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: They had them but I didn't hear too much of them. I've heard more of them since he's been dead. I should have... I could have heard a lot more of them if I had been interested in those things but I wasn't. He was just there and that was...

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: A fixture is the idea. Really part of the town, part of the town. He was just a character and...

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: And at that time I was... I was too busy I couldn't be bothered.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about Pete Olsen?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: You knew him rather well. I knew him very well too. Well I always felt pretty sorry for Pete because he always looked like he needed a bath. But he was an omnivorous reader. And he was a smart fellow. But he was... He was pretty much of a... I guess you'd call him a loner. He didn't... His three dogs and his two horses or was it...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he have three horses?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: They seemed to be his whole life. All that he was interested in. But he was really a good fellow. I know he'd take my Sunday school kids on sleigh rides and... And I don't think anybody else around town would for nothing.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: And... He came to a rather tragic end. Got drunk and fell in the ditch. Woke up dead. One cold night. But he'd gotten to be a terrible white elephant. But he slept after we left there. He was in pretty good shape when we left Louisville. I've been gone from there for 22 years.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: Well with this reading that he did, you say I'm a nivorous...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where do you even get his material from?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: I don't know.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: When I say he was a nivorous reader, what makes me think that... I think you read the Almanac. I mean I repeat that from A to Izzard. And I went into his place one time. And he never threw the newspapers away. He must have read them over and over and reread them. They were stacked about that high. And I don't know why he'd keep them unless he was going to read them again. And he was...

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: Oh he... I don't know what books he'd get ahold of. If he ever did get ahold of books. But I know there was anything in the paper that he hadn't read. And he was quite interested in... I know one time he was telling me how long it took the leaves that fell from the trees to build up the earth. How many centuries it had taken for the earth to be covered with soil. So he had to have done some reading to have figured out that the earth was replenished by the falling leaves every year. And the dead animals and things.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was his house like?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: Don't ask me. His house was a mess. It was a little grey house and on the side was a lean -to. And he had an old Ford I guess in there. And it was just piled high with junk. And you went into his house, got into the kitchen. I never saw his bedroom. And here the papers were piled way up. And another box was full of tin cans. And garbage. And I don't think the floor had been swept for a hundred years. And his house was... Well you just can't describe it unless you've seen it. He wouldn't have thought a pack rack would have had a happy home there. But maybe it would. His house was really something. And he was pretty healthy. All that dirt didn't affect his health. And he was always around every day.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: I've heard that he did some pretty rugged jobs around Bovill.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you mention that to me?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: I think when we talked. I know that hotel in Bovill, that big grey one over there in the one case. He used the one that crawled under there. And put a foundation under it and come out all muddy from here down. And jobs that nobody else would take while they got Pete to do it. And he would put foundations under houses. And do just a lot of things like that. But that was the worst job I know that they had, was putting foundation under that house. And that he just had to be like a mole to burrow under there. And raise those floors, because the floors were just like that inside.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that how he made his living, doing odd jobs for the town of Bovill?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: Uh huh. And he had a little farm up on the hill where he raised hay. But we always said he worked like a dog all summer to raise that hay. For the horses to eat all winter and then he never got a hand. Horses were not disciplined. I suppose they could pull a load. And he made, I know he made shakes, but I don't know whether he sold them or not.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he use his horses to work very much?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: I don't know what he, I suppose he plowed gardens, he must have. But there weren't many gardens in Bovill and everything froze so badly. But he was, he loved his horses and his dogs.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: I don't imagine he had any, I wonder if he had people to talk to about these interests of his from reading. I can't imagine too many people were interested in reading the way you say it was.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: I don't know whether he did or not. He was always busy, you know. Always, when you'd see him he'd be walking along real fast and didn't have much time to stop.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: And I think he must have had some books around somewhere because otherwise he'd, I don't know how he knew so much unless he did a lot of reading.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: He got the idea that the old lady that ran me a drugstore was trying to marry him. Of course that was after he'd gotten to be quite a whino. We got a laugh over that because she hated, she said she was an old maid, but she liked men anyway I think. But she didn't like, I think she hated men really.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: She didn't like, she said she didn't like Pete very much though.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: No, because she hated him a lot, but she didn't have anything particular against him. She had a big stock, silver string on her and take pay. I know two of them had a big Buick. And when he left that Buick was just crammed with all of her lovely things. She had things in that drugstore you'd find in Spokane.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What's that? Good store up there?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: Ball Marche. Oh, better than Ball Marche. It was a drugstore. Cosmetics and glassware.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: She had really good things.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: Excellent things. High price stuff. And she had quite a jewelry display too. And her pearls would be those good name pearls. You know they wouldn't be just a string of beads. And one fella had his car just loaded to the hill with that. I bet he had Christmas presents for the next fifty years. And then probably the stock would turn out to be no good.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: I think one's burned twice shy on something like that. But all the stockers could get around here.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: And they'd do it. But she did now too. And they better not. Well I guess it's the second hand store in there. Where her drugstore was now.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well most of the other teachers in Bovill, single women also, when you were first there in the early years, were they married?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: One of the high school teachers was married. Well they had four high school teachers because one of them was married and the superintendent were married. It was Mr. and Ms. Holbrook.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: But we downstairs weren't married. None of the great people were married except the eighth grade teacher. He was married. He was married. And for years there were no married teachers. But long towards the last one he was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they work mostly?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: Uh oh. One woman had a couple of daughters so I think she had a little house that she lived in. But I think that was after my time. And the other woman, another woman that was married and they lived in the house. She and her husband. But for a long time they didn't hire anybody if they were Catholic or if they were married. That's the way it was when I went there. Because one of the men on the board didn't like Catholics. But when he got off the board there were Catholic girls teaching there too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What's the problem with hiring a married woman to teach?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: Well there wasn't any except they probably figured the old maids needed to make a living too. Needed to eat too.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: But eventually they hired married women.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When did you get married?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: Not until after I finished teaching. I was all the way I got married I quit.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have to or did you? Would you have had to quit if you?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: No I don't think so but I've been there nine years and I thought nine years was about enough.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: Well I taught a year after I was married but that was a year or two later during the second world war when they couldn't get a teacher I took the school for a year. But I substituted in, after I was married I substituted in Elk River and Derry and in Bobo too when they got in a bind.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you were teaching that in the years you were teaching what were the main activities that you did besides teaching? Did you do church school work there?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And then socially it was clubs mostly?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: Card clubs. And dancing. And there was nothing else to do.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: In Bobo you either you either were helped in the church and played cards or else you were a barfly and I was not a barfly. I never hung around the bar stall.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: And then every time they'd have a drive for cancer or the heart or something like that why they'd stick us on it and we'd have, well I did that after I quit teaching. Do it for lives.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there much of a difference in status and different social status in different groups of people in Bobo?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: I know I've heard it mentioned, I'm trying to think of who the, I've heard it mentioned a few of the ladies that were sort of upper crust.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: Well I think there was. Mr. Naugle's wife and Mrs. Mallory and Mrs. Ellison and Mrs. Parker and Mrs. Cryer.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: The people in town that had the businesses were the upper crust and they'd let the schoolteachers kind of be on the outer circle. But of course after I was married by then I was just as good as the rest of them. Well the dogs used to go on camping trips together. So I guess we all, but then there was another bunch of younger people who had their own group too. But they were the ones that spent their time down at the bars. And none of the group I mentioned were interested in being in the bars. Of course, Allison's ran one, but they had the hotel before that. And Denavins too, they were elite.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: It sounds like the elite was both the businesses and some of the foremen and their families. Yes, and the Cranes were in the, they were nice people too.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: And of course the elite, they were the elite and then they were not so elite.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who were logging and that, just the regular loggers?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: No, the loggers were just, they were the people too. The not, well the not solely to be the ones that were drunkards or well drunkards I guess, the only people that were beyond the pale.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: Oh I see, so what you're saying is that those people were beneath the ordinary people.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: You felt sorry for them, but you didn't have the environment for dinner.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: Well I know that it's usual in lumber towns, and especially among lumberjack there's a lot of drinking. I mean it's very common.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: It almost seems like part of the life for old time lumberjack anyway.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: Well it's the same thing here in most of them. There's people that are way up in the social scale that are not equal to drugs too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well did prohibition and just drive it all underground just kept on going on the same way when there was just moonshine and no order to carry on?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: Well there wasn't so much drinking in Louisville until after they got beer and then of course it got to be a lot of beer. The people had drank far too much beer for their food, but after beer came in one of them some people could get very much inebriated on that stuff.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: There wasn't too much, and it looked like whiskey around there. Of course I wasn't around where I know it anyway. I was up there peddling ABC's all day.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: Better for your health than whiskey, that's for sure.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there much of a sentiment against drinking among some of the people?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: The good Presbyterians thought it was terrible. They wouldn't touch a drop, but there weren't too many. There were just a few of them.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: There was a big liberal crowd that believed in living and letting them. They did just their own business.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: One other person that I always heard in the elite of the town was Mrs.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: Jones.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: Well, her day had passed by the time I got there.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: She was around, but she hadn't any following at all. She had ceased giving parties or associating with any of the rabble. She sat up there in her house on the hill when she was there. They had an eastern star in a goble, but she didn't join it. I don't know whether anybody suggested that she transferred a goble or not, but anyway she never did. But that was before my time. She was supposed to be the global queen, but her reign had gone long before I got there. Because when I got there, he'd been a lot of lawsuits over some of his ventures, and he turned all his money over to her. When he came downtown to buy anything, you'd have to find out how much it cost and then go back and get the money from her.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Aunt?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: Aunt Jones.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was her first thing?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: He'd have to go back and get the money from her.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: She doled it out?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: She doled it out, nickel and dime at a time. If he'd come down to a food sale that we'd have and see a pie or something that he wanted, he'd have to go back home and get the money to buy him. Oh, Lord.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He didn't have little small changes?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: No, sir. But then she got nuts before she died. She was crazy.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: She still stayed in Bovill, though?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: Well, she stayed there I guess till she died. If you want to get the low down on Mrs. T. P. Jones, one of the women that worked for her, there were two of them, had to go up and take care of her because she got pretty rabid.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: And, oh, she did something with an axe one time. I'd chop down the door, get somebody with a butcher knife or something. She really lost her. I don't know why. I never was in her house but once.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: Well, she had a reputation for really liking to hold onto money before, I think even before T. P. Jones retired. She seemed to have the feeling that I've gotten from the way people have talked about her is that she liked to run things and run the money and be in charge, which she could. If she could be in charge, she wouldn't have much to do with it.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: I know she got very much incensed one time. She used to come to church because some twelve -year -old kid took communion. She didn't like that. Well, it's nothing to me if a two -year -old takes communion. But, oh, she was, every once in a while she'd buy a new set of song books for the church. I got a, I think, I think 1950 she bought a new song book. And she bought new song books once before. I guess they got too ragged suited in. But, those days they didn't cost too much.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: Well, I wonder if her influence waned after T. P.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: Jones stopped working for the company, if that had much to do with it. All these lawsuits, they were gone out of town quite a bit. They were, I don't know where they lived, London, I guess. But she never, I never saw her as a social function in the only place I ever saw her was in church. Oh, I saw her coming down to the post office to get her mail and stuff like that. But, she never went to anything that I ever went to. Of course, I was teaching school. There could have been a lot of parties I wasn't at. But, I'm sure she wasn't there either.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you came to Beauville in the late 20s, did you consider it a real lively town?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: No, I didn't. I thought it was kind of developed. Every Friday as soon as school was out, I'd get my little Model A and I'd head for St. Mary's or Nez Perce or Lewiston or Spokane or something. I hadn't spent many weekends there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you, what would you do in towns like, in those towns?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: Oh, I had friends there.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: Two of my friends were teaching up at St. Joe's. And we spent the weekend together. And I had friends, and my folks lived at Nez Perce. And I had friends down here at Lewiston. And relatives in Spokane, so I didn't have to stay in town. Unless I wanted to. Beauville used to have a dance on Halloween, although there was something. We'd send a Spokane to some costume company for our costumes yet. And it was pretty hard to guess who was in some of those costumes that came down.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was that? Theatrical Costuming Place up there?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: I've heard of them. Seems to me it cost us ten dollars to rent a costume to go to that dance.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Ten dollars a piece?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: Uh -huh. For the costume. See, they'd ship it down from Spokane and you'd have to mail it back. And I believe the rent was ten dollars.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you get the big one you wanted?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: Yeah, you'd write up and tell them who you wanted to be. I know one couple came as two big owls. And there was no possible way in the world to tell who those owls were because there was, I suppose, a paper mache thing with feathers pasted on it, but they were good owls. And there'd be Sir Walter Raleigh and Queen Elizabeth and the Dracula, just name it. That costumer had it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is this in the early days?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: Well, it was when I first went there. The day when they had those, I don't remember them being more than two or three years. Of course, they could have been more often. I'd forgotten. That's a long way back for me to remember. Yeah, it's fifty years. Yeah. And I never did dwell on the past much. Tomorrow's more interesting.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That's a good attitude, but even so, this transportation in those days, your Model A, how good were the roads then?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: Oh, they were gravel.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: And they weren't very wide. I know the first time I drove to St. Mary's, the girls came down on the train from St. Joe to show me how to get up there. So I drove back to St. Joe, and I was a pretty good driver, and I guess it must have been kind of fast. And those girls scared P. Green, and they told me I sure came fast that the St. Joe River was right down there on my right, I guess. I don't know what side it was. And gee, I sure, when I drove back to Sunday and saw that road, it was just two wheel tracks wide. I didn't mean anybody, I guess. But if I did, I met them soon enough to get by. But they sure scared me going back. And those days you could drive down from St. Mary's towards the by then. Sometimes I'd be pretty sleepy, and I'd go off the side of the road and try to sleep. And one time when I was going down the night, I could hear those, I thought it was a hyena howl, some kind of a bobcat, I guess it was. But they cry like women, you know, like a baby. Cougars do that. Cougars, that's what I was trying to say. I've heard cougars several times.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You'd just pull off the side of the road and sleep for a while?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: Well, that would be in the daytime, would it be half. But when I'd be caught up there and have to drive back in the dark, I'd keep right on going.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: I wouldn't think of stopping a car and taking a nap now with all the freaks that are running around.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, in winter, didn't Bovill pretty much put its cars up and forget about them?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: I'll say they did.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: We had a very bad strip of road. They called it Hog Meadows. And if you got through Hog Meadows and got into town while you never went out, one year I came to Nespers for Christmas. And I left my car in a snow bank over there by Nespers, though, so other people had deep snow, too.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: But we'd put our cars up in the winter and they'd stay there until spring. And Hubert Hall was always the first one in his car, over the hill to Clarkville. And that, of course, he'd put the rest of us on wait two or three weeks before we tried it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the idea?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: Oh, just to be the first.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: When they pointed at it all.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did things change dramatically when the Depression hit Bovill? Was there sudden change or was it gradual and what was the change?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: Well, it was, I don't remember about that, but it sure was a great leveler. There wasn't, only two or three people had any money during the Depression.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: The rest of us just scratched along.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: But, you know, the Depression was kind of bad because that bank in Bovill took the people's check the night before they put out the sign the next morning the bank was closed. That was a dirty trade. Because they knew that. I think it closed the second of February. It was groundhog's day. And they took the fellow's paychecks and that was it. I think we got about ten cents on the dollar back.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: Great.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: So they didn't catch much money in the bank. Those days you had stockings, you know, and it had a seam up the back and then this big thing that folded over to make the stocking top for your passenger garters had a slit in it about that wide. And I kept my money on top of my sock. So this had the money in your sock was really true because if I hadn't had my money in my sock I'd have been in a bad way. You pulled it out of the bank right away then. Well, I always kept pretty well spent up, you know, and pay my rent and my food and buy some clothes and gas and stuff. And you see it was right after Christmas. So February when it closed so I didn't have much money in. But I didn't have any. Well, I may have had some in but I didn't have a great deal. My brother had some in too.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: But a lot of people had their money down at the Folloch Bank. They went down and got it out. But I guess it didn't close.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What happened when once the Depression hit and the company pulled the pin like they say and the men were out of work?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: Oh, nothing. They all that's when they had this. I guess it was Red Cross flour and Red Cross sugar and beans and I think overall stuff like that. Down at Parker's store and the people that were hard up could go down and get it. The people with large families would go down and get some. And then Mr. Naugle was the chief of the potlatch and tow and he would hire some of the men to shovel the sidewalks and shovel roofs and things like that. So they had a little bit of money.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What else did they do?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: They had WPA then too. And WPA, I think put in some new water pipes or something.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: It is a road building I think.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: That's what somebody told me. But it was a big help then too. Brought all those kids out of it. The East sent our kids, I don't know where the local kids went. Some of them I guess were in the campground building.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The presence of those kids from the East make itself felt in the town?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: Oh yes. They would be up at the dances and dance with all the girls. One of the girls married one of them went back to New Jersey and lived. I visited her there one time. She died this summer though so I won't go back.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: And one or two of the CC's never went back East. They stayed there. One of them married one of the Sanderson girls along the runs of restaurant. I can't think of her name.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What is name on it?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: But that helped the economy quite a bit.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think there was much actual suffering? I mean real deprivation during those days for some of the people in town?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: I don't think there was in Boho because if anybody heard of anyone being out of food, there would be food taken into them.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: There was one family that was reported to be sleeping on the floor. They took in bedding and mattresses and stuff. I don't know why they should have been sleeping on the floor if they were there before the depression started. One of our local men, Doc Granis, bought books for the kids that couldn't afford them. Somebody else bought over shoes for one family with eight kids. They helped each other out then. They had to really get hungry and then they got free flour and stuff. And then, you know, there was no reason. And then if they could get boats, they could go out and get deer. They could get venison. I don't know whether they were very plentiful then or not. So they had, I think most people managed to have meat.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: And there were a lot of people cut wood, sold it, to get extra dollars because there were lots of wood stoves and gold in them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that the togetherness of the community was a lot stronger than during the depression than it was before? Or was it the same? Was it always there?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: Oh, I think it was probably there, but then it wasn't so evident because it seemed to me it was all of us were doing the same things after the depression as we did before. Only we probably didn't do them in quite a gram manner.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: If we had a party, we probably had a very light lunch. And if it hadn't been a depression, we'd probably have nuts and candy and whipped cream and sandwiches and dessert and everything.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: Probably have a...

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: Bogle used to have fun putting on plays too. Community plays.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: They never tried anything, any Shakespeare or anything like that. It'd usually be a... farce of some sort. Comedy. And it would really fill a house. Sometimes they'd take a place, I think they took it to El Quirrell and Sir Derry, one of the places that... I wasn't in that one. Didn't have any to it.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: But they put on a play one that's called The Patsy.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: And then I coached one called, I think it's Hiram Slick from Pumpkin Creek. And that was a farce, but it was a lot of fun and well received.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: And they put on the old maids convention once. A bunch of us women put on a... from Rebecca Lodge put on a play. I think there were seven characters. Four of them were women and three were men. And since I know I was one of the men, somebody else. Two of the other women were men. That was a lot of fun.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: We had a... a dog had to come in on the scene. It came in another time. The gal had to open up a bottle of something and...

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: Hubert had shaken it, you know, when she opened it. It spewed all over everybody.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were they supposed to happen?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: No. It was supposed to be just open and be... I think it was great just for something, but he got something in there all over. And another time they had one of those plays. They had to have a lunch. And they spiked the stuff that these actors were drinking. That was kind of funny.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: During the play?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: Uh -huh. They didn't do it at rehearsal, but they did it the main night.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: That was fun.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: Sounds like the plays were much more important than in a lot of communities. I didn't know they had...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: they had some, any?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: Well, they had...

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: they just had them by spurts, you know. There was one fellow in town. Neelan Parker had been an actor. And he had written radio plays down... they were on KGO. And he'd been on a tour to the Orient. At the time of the Japanese earthquake with a traveling troop. So when... the winters he'd be in town, well, there'd always be a play because he'd seek to it. And then maybe the next year I'd correct one or somebody else would.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did he do in town?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: Oh, he just come... he was a narrative well. He... he would just come to town, put in his time until he went off on another to do something else. I don't know how he made his living. But he had great promise that a mother to nothing spoiled Mama's boy.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He liked Bovill though, huh?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: Yeah. It was a good place to rest up. Then he had fun putting on these plays. He put on... I don't know the one he put on was the Patsy and I can't remember what the other was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, did he spend other time in big cities and that kind of thing?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: Oh, yes.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: He would be around San Francisco and Portland. But I... I know one night I heard... I heard one of the plays that he had written over KGO myself, so I know he did it. And besides that, his... we had clippings and stuff, so we knew he had... he was an actor. Good looking. He was a handsome room. I wonder why he picked Bovill as his place. Well, his parents lived there. I see. His parents had the store. He used to go to the University of Idaho years and years ago. Was this the E .K.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Parker store?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Whatever happened to the man?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: He finally died. He's buried up there in the Moscow Cemetery.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were there movies that regularly come to Bovill?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: Oh, yes. The Den of... Mr. Den of... had a picture show and it ran every night for years and they...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This is Lucille Den of his husband?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: Father. Father -in -law. And I'm sure there was a show every night.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: I could be wrong about that, but I think there was a show every night. But anyway, they would have these serials that continued, you know, like... Don't think it was called The Perils of Pauline, but a long time ago they'd have these shows where... the heroine would be tied on the tracks, the engine would be coming down to run over and she'd be saved at the last minute. And they had those things every... I think it was every Sunday night and then a feature besides. And then there used to be...

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: theatrical troupes come through town and rent the hall and have plays too. I've heard of that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they call that Chautauqua's?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: No. Something else. The Chautauqua was a thing. They had Chautauqua's in Bovill too, but the Chautauqua was put on in a tent. They'd always bring their own tent, but these were traveling troupes. Now, the Malon players.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you ever hear of them?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: Well, they used to come to Bovill all the time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I think they were out of Spokane, weren't they?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: I don't know, but I have heard the name. And it seems to me that Neil enacted with the Malon players a while, but he may not have. And they would make Bovill. And that went on for several years. And then I don't know why they quit coming. I guess the beer parlors opened up and the people would rather drink beer.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: For a town that you say the town was sort of dead. It sounds like there was something going on in it. Oh, yeah, there was, I guess.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: But then that was over a period of many years, you know. But just a day at a time, sometimes it didn't seem very exciting.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that Bovill was a very close town, that people in the town were very close to each other as compared to, let's say, some of the farming towns or other places?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: I don't know.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: I never lived in a farming town, the only place. Well, I guess I did when I was a little kid, but I don't remember anything about that.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: They said that the people said Bovill was off of clicking.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I guess that means close, doesn't it?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: Well, that's close, but that's not close for everybody.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: There was a lot more, a great deal more closeness among people in those days and communities than there is nowadays. And I don't know whether or not that's really true, but maybe people idealized the past, but it does seem...

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: And last night, the police car was over on that corner for a long, long time. I don't know why they were there. Now, in a small town, we'd have been out and we'd know why. But here, in a town this size, you just don't pay attention to it. But if anything like that had happened in a town of five or six hundredth, the whole town would have been gathered around to see what was happening.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: I think the urban community is self -centered.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you mean?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: Well, I mean, they're all for me, fully on you.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: Because time is going so fast and people have got so much to do, and so little time to do it in that they don't consider the other person much anymore. And when we lived in this lower state, Pace, we were more interested in our fellow man.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: And I think if we can't run our cars and can't keep our houses warm, the people are going to get a lot more friendly, too. They're going to go over to somebody who's got a good fireplace.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Are you suggesting by that that hard times help bring people together?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: You betcha.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: They surely do.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: It'll either bring them together or the have -nots will get something that the haves are going to have. Zita got to bring them together or bring revolution because it's not fair for some people to have it all and the rest of us to sit around shiver.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you think the attitude of people in Beauville was towards the Potlatch Lumber Company?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: I was just talking with Mrs. Cryer a week or two ago, and the attitude of the Beauville people toward the Potlatch Lumber Company in the early days, that it was Papa Potlatch or Uncle Potlatch. Because before they became wire -houses, they were just community operation. But after they became wire -houses, a lot of the closest disappeared. I know this lady said the other day, she just hated to hear people say mean things about the Lumber Company. It doesn't bother me any. That's off of them.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: Because the Potlatch used to send the turkeys to the people at Christmastime, and they did to us, but now it's wire -houses. They don't get any Christmas turkey anymore.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You mean to the people in Beauville?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: To the, yeah, not to the foreman. Well, I think just a... I don't know whether everybody got them or not, but of course we didn't get them until after we moved down here. And the urban men belonged to some kind of an organization they had, and we'd get our Christmas turkey and our Christmas tree. But not any... those things won't happen anymore. And when the first one, well I guess they still do, the Potlatch would go out and get the Christmas tree for our church. When we couldn't get anybody else to. We had that man named Ritzheimer there. All we had to do was ask, and we received. But I didn't know the Potlatch was so generous, or I'd have done a lot of asking in those twenty -five years. But I didn't do.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What would people ask for from Ritzheimer?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: Oh, for a Christmas tree, or could... they needed some lumber for some benches in the gym, or they needed some paint to do something somewhere. The Potlatch would give it to them. Ritzheimer, of course, came out of the Potlatch, but down here I get a big kick out of... The Potlatch gave so much to the, this driver, that driver's for the drive. Potlatch, my eye.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They take it out of the man's check, don't they? Is that the Potlatch giving it? Or the people?

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: Maybe I'm wrong. No, I think you're undoubtedly right.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: It's...

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: they set aside so much for that kind of thing.

speaker: Marie Leitch Fisher
words: But it seems to... but I know that they get these things taken out of their check, so I don't see how much they get the credit for. Maybe they don't. I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
